title: Hali Meiðhad
summary: The repository XML is a local copy/version of University of Michigan CME item HMaid. U-M scanned and OCRed Frederick James Furnivall's posthumously published 1922 revision of EETS Original Series 18. That edition prints parallel transcriptions from Oxford, Bodleian Library MS Bodley 34, fols. 52v–71v, and British Library Cotton MS Titus D XVIII, fols. 112v–127r, and revises/expands Oswald Cockayne's 1866 edition of the Cotton witness. The XML encodes the two witnesses as separate divisions and omits the title page, prefatory note, grammatical notes, and glossary. A 1960 printed facsimile of Bodley 34 and the 2016 METS edition are linked as comparison resources, not upstream sources of HMaid.xml.
review_notes: The two DIV1 elements represent two witnesses of one work; they must not be modeled as two separate authored works.
The medieval text, nineteenth- and early-twentieth-century editions, U-M encoding, modern scholarly editions, and manuscript photographs have separate rights and access layers.

entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library MS Bodley 34
entities.description: Early-thirteenth-century manuscript containing the complete Katherine Group; Hali Meiðhad occupies fols. 52v–71v.

date_statements.label: Manuscript production
date_statements.value: First quarter of the thirteenth century, approximately 1225

notes: The Middle English text is laid out as prose across the page.

entities.label: British Library Cotton MS Titus D XVIII
entities.description: Composite parchment codex; its early-thirteenth-century Katherine Group part contains Hali Meiðhad at fols. 112v–127r.

date_statements.label: Katherine Group manuscript part
date_statements.value: First half of the thirteenth century

holding.notes: Hali Meiðhad is at fols. 112v–127r in the part comprising fols. 14–147.

physical_description.extent: Composite codex; relevant part fols. 14–147; Hali Meiðhad fols. 112v–127r
physical_description.format: Gothic script, two columns of 24–30 lines
notes: Initials are green with red pen flourishing; paraphs are red.

entities.label: Facsimile of MS. Bodley 34, EETS Original Series 247
entities.description: Printed facsimile of Bodley 34 with an introduction by N. R. Ker; useful for comparing manuscript layout and the faint Hali Meiðhad rubric.

subject.description: The complete local HMaid XML artifact

scope.notes: The local file carries U-M's title, DLPS identifier, publication and encoding statements, and two witness divisions. The exact repository ingestion date is not documented.

object.description: The Bodley and Cotton parallel texts and their inline editorial notes

scope.notes: The XML header says the title page, prefatory note, grammatical notes, and glossarial index were not encoded.

object.description: Cockayne's 1866 EETS OS 18 edition from Cotton Titus D XVIII

scope.notes: The 1922 edition revises the earlier edition and adds a parallel Bodley 34 text; it is not a simple reprint.

subject.description: The Cotton Titus D XVIII parallel text
The Hali Meithhad edition and translation

scope.notes: This is a related modern edition for comparison, not an upstream source of HMaid.xml.

access.access_method: Read the checked-out CME submodule path.
Read the item and either witness division online.
Use either full-view copy linked from the HathiTrust catalog record.
access.format: Full-view page images; Google Play also lists a free ebook

access.access_method: Open the BSB digitization through Deutsche Digitale Bibliothek or the stable BSB URN; use the exact IIIF manifest for all page images.

local_copies.label: Complete BSB Cockayne Hali Meidenhad 1866 IIIF bundle
notes: ZIP contains the exact BSB presentation manifest, an exact-source inventory, and all 60 full-size canvas images in the complete digital object.
The full digitized volume is retained alongside the independent Internet Archive scan; it is not reduced to the edition's text pages.
Local caching does not override the provider's No Copyright-Non-Commercial Use Only statement.

work_portion.label: Cockayne's Middle English text with facing modernization
notes: Manifest labels and page inspection locate printed p. 1 at canvas 11 and the final Middle English text at printed p. 47/canvas 57; the glossary begins on the following pages.
The provider applies No Copyright–Non-Commercial Use Only to the digital surrogate; link rather than redistribute its images in a commercial-capable corpus unless separate permission applies.

access.access_method: Internet Archive item and exact direct PDF download

local_copies.label: Cockayne Hali Meidenhad 1866 complete scan
notes: Complete 58-page provider PDF of Cockayne's printed volume, including title matter, foreword, parallel alternating modernization and Middle English transcription, glossary, and terminal matter.

work_portion.label: Cockayne's Middle English text with facing modernization
locators: Middle English transcription on alternating pages 11-55
notes: Direct inspection confirms the modernized presentation begins on PDF page 10, the Middle English transcription on PDF page 11, and the final Middle English text on PDF page 55; the glossary begins on PDF page 57.
The Internet Archive item supplies no verified item-level reuse license; caching is not an independent reuse grant.

access.access_method: Apply for a Bodleian Reader card with Group A access, state the research interest, and order the manuscript for consultation at the Weston Library. Independent researchers are explicitly welcomed.
access.format: Onsite consultation in a Weston Library reading room

access.access_method: Complete the Bodleian mediated-copying order form for reference-quality PDF scans; identify shelfmark MS. Bodl. 34 and fols. 52v–71v. Contact the service first if scan feasibility or foliation is uncertain.
access.format: Reference-quality PDF scans with simple shelfmark/folio metadata
notes: The public page states £1 per scan for non-Oxford users and an average turnaround of 10 working days, subject to legal, conservation, and other exceptions.
Images are supplied subject to Imaging Services Terms of Use; obtain separate written terms for repository publication or print-on-demand reuse.

access.access_method: Register or renew a British Library Reader Pass, use the linked online collection-item request form, quote Cotton MS Titus D XVIII, and consult manuscripts in London. The catalog says a letter of introduction is required.
access.contact: British Library Reference Services via the catalog's Reference team link

access.access_method: The parent catalog points to the legacy Digitised Manuscripts object but explicitly says the digital images are currently unavailable.
access.format: Legacy digital manuscript images, currently unavailable
notes: This is a dated service-status statement, not a claim that no digital surrogate exists. Recheck as British Library systems recover.

access.access_method: Consult the printed facsimile at the Folger Shakespeare Library; the catalog lists an open-stacks copy.
Read the edited Middle English text and translation online or use METS download/print features subject to provider terms.
access.format: HTML scholarly edition and translation with notes

rights.component: The HMaid materials presented through U-M's CME item
rights.basis: U-M currently labels the materials public domain and applies the DPLA No Copyright–United States statement.
notes: No Copyright–United States is a rights statement, not a worldwide license and not a statement about manuscript photography.

rights.component: The checked-in XML transcription and structure derived from U-M's encoding of the 1922 edition
rights.basis: U-M currently applies No Copyright–United States to HMaid, and the immediate printed source was published in 1922, before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff.
notes: The XML embeds a historical U-M availability notice allowing individual copying for personal use, research, and teaching while forbidding redistribution by content providers. That historical notice conflicts in scope with the current public-domain statement and should be preserved and clarified rather than silently discarded.
Repository-authored code, metadata, typesetting, and generated outputs can have separate licenses.

rights.component: The 1922 edition, parallel transcriptions, modernization, notes, and other published editorial matter
rights.basis: The edition was published in 1922. U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A states that all works published in the United States before 1931 are in the public domain as of 2026.
notes: Provider terms can still govern use of a particular scan or interface even when the underlying edition is public domain.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1866, far before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff.

rights.component: The BSB/DDB digital surrogate and supplied page images
rights.basis: The Deutsche Digitale Bibliothek record labels the digitization No Copyright–Non-Commercial Use Only.
notes: NoC-NC is a rights statement, not an affirmative open license. It is safer to link to these images than redistribute them in a corpus that supports commercial print-on-demand use.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan images and PDF derivative for the 1866 edition
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: The underlying 1866 edition is public domain in the United States, but the exact Internet Archive item exposes no item-level scan-image license or rights field.
notes: Public anonymous download and local caching do not themselves grant redistribution rights in the scan files.

rights.component: The medieval textual content embodied in Bodley 34
rights.basis: The manuscript and its texts date from the early thirteenth century and are centuries beyond any U.S. copyright term.
notes: This concerns medieval content, not modern photographs, scans, catalog prose, or order terms.

rights.component: The medieval textual content embodied in Cotton Titus D XVIII
rights.basis: The relevant manuscript part dates from the first half of the thirteenth century and is centuries beyond any U.S. copyright term.
notes: This concerns medieval content, not modern photographs, scans, catalog prose, or reader conditions.

rights.component: Any new scans supplied by Bodleian mediated copying or Imaging Services
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: The service supplies scans subject to Imaging Services Terms of Use; the public order page does not grant item-specific repository-publication or commercial-reuse permission.
notes: Ask for written reuse terms before committing supplied scans or using them in a print-on-demand publication.

rights.component: British Library digital manuscript photographs or scans
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: The catalog reports that legacy digital images are currently unavailable and supplies no item-specific image-reuse grant on the manuscript record.

rights.component: The 1960 facsimile's photographs, N. R. Ker's introduction, layout, and other modern contributions
rights.basis: The facsimile was published in 1960 and includes modern photographs and an introduction; no open license was found, and the status of the U.S. publication formalities/renewal has not been researched.
notes: A holding-library catalog and onsite access do not authorize republication of the facsimile images.

rights.component: The modern edition, translation, introduction, notes, glosses, and web presentation
rights.basis: The edition was published in 2016 and is a modern scholarly and translated presentation; free web access is not treated as public-domain status or a blanket republication grant.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Two manuscript witnesses and a reader-oriented modernization
editorial_practices.representation: Bodley and Cotton texts printed in parallel on facing pages, with a modernized version at the foot of pages
editorial_practices.description: The 1922 edition enables witness comparison while retaining a separate modernization; it is a revised expansion of Cockayne's Cotton-only edition.
editorial_practices.scope: The complete work in both witnesses

editorial_practices.source_feature: The two parallel witness texts in Furnivall's source edition
editorial_practices.representation: Separate DIV1 nodes HMaid:1 for Bodley 34 and HMaid:2 for Cotton Titus D XVIII
editorial_practices.description: U-M preserves separately addressable witness transcriptions rather than silently conflating their readings into one normalized text.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Title page, prefatory note, grammatical notes, and glossarial index in the 1922 edition
editorial_practices.description: U-M's encoding declaration explicitly identifies these omitted edition components.
editorial_practices.scope: Front matter, back matter, and ancillary apparatus outside the two transcribed witness texts
notes: HMaid.xml is not a complete digital facsimile or complete-content transcription of the 1922 volume.

editorial_practices.source_feature: The work's two surviving manuscript witnesses
editorial_practices.representation: The 1866 edition represents Cotton Titus D XVIII only
editorial_practices.description: The first EETS edition was made from the Cotton witness; Bodley 34 was added in Furnivall's later parallel-text revision.
editorial_practices.scope: Hali Meidenhad in Cotton Titus D XVIII

evidence.summary: The file identifies U-M's 1997 encoding, Furnivall's 1922 revision of Cockayne's EETS edition, Bodley 34 and Cotton Titus D XVIII as sources, U-M's OCR/markup workflow and omissions, and two witness-level divisions.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, Hali Meidenhad item information.
evidence.summary: The page identifies Furnivall, Oxford University Press 1922, separate Bodley and Cotton contents, and U-M's public-domain/No Copyright–United States statement.

evidence.citation: National Library of Ireland, Hali meidenhad: an alliterative homily of the thirteenth century, 1922.
evidence.summary: The record identifies the 1922 volume as Furnivall's revision of Cockayne's EETS OS 18, based on Bodley 34 and Cotton Titus D XVIII, with parallel texts and a modernized version at the foot of pages.

evidence.citation: HathiTrust Digital Library, catalog record 001644594, Hali meidenhad, 1922.
evidence.summary: The record gives the 1922 publication and 116-page extent and links two full-view digital copies.

evidence.citation: Google Play Books, Hali meidenhad: an alliterative homily of the thirteenth century, 1922.
evidence.summary: Google Play lists the 1922 116-page EETS volume by Furnivall and Cockayne as a free ebook.

evidence.citation: Deutsche Digitale Bibliothek / Bayerische Staatsbibliothek, Oswald Cockayne, Hali meidenhad, 1866.
evidence.locator: Bibliographic record, download, and rights statement
evidence.summary: The record identifies Cockayne, Cotton Titus D XVIII fol. 112c, Trübner 1866, EETS OS 18, the BSB digitization, and a No Copyright–Non-Commercial Use Only provider statement.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive, cuahalimeidenhad00cock, complete scan of Cockayne's Hali meidenhad (1866).
evidence.locator: Complete 58-page PDF; edited presentation at PDF pp. 10-55
evidence.summary: The exact direct PDF was retrieved and inspected. It preserves Cockayne's title matter, foreword, alternating modernized and Middle English texts, glossary, and terminal matter; the item metadata provides no item-level rights or license field for the scan.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Cotton MS Titus D XVIII, ff. 14–147.
evidence.locator: Contents, date, access, and physical characteristics
evidence.summary: The record places Hali Meiðhad at fols. 112v–127r, dates the part to 1200–1249, records Middle English and Latin, describes parchment, Gothic script and two-column layout, and explains current physical request requirements.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Cotton MS Titus D XVIII.
evidence.locator: Composite contents, physical description, and Digitised Content status
evidence.summary: The parent record describes the composite codex and explicitly reports that legacy digital images are currently unavailable.

evidence.citation: Emily Rebekah Huber and Elizabeth Robertson, eds. and trans., The Katherine Group (MS Bodley 34), Middle English Texts Series, 2016.
evidence.locator: Edition description, credits, classification, and contents
evidence.summary: The page identifies the editors/translators and 2016 publication and classifies the Bodley 34 group as thirteenth-century Middle English prose, including Hali Meithhad (Holy Maidenhood).

evidence.citation: Emily Rebekah Huber and Elizabeth Robertson, Introduction to The Katherine Group (MS Bodley 34), Middle English Texts Series.
evidence.locator: Bodley 34, Hali Meithhad, audience, region, and textual modes sections
evidence.summary: The introduction describes Bodley 34's date, size, contents and Welsh-Marches context, Hali Meithhad's subject and Latin sources, probable female contemplative audience, and the editors' decision to present the rhythmical alliterative texts as prose.

evidence.citation: Huber and Robertson, Hali Meithhad (Holy Maidenhood), Middle English Texts Series.
evidence.locator: Complete edited text and manuscript-folio sequence
evidence.summary: The online edition presents the Bodley 34 text with folio progression from 52v through 71v and provides modern scholarly notes and translation.

evidence.locator: Who can access special collections and planning a visit
evidence.summary: The page explicitly welcomes independent researchers, explains Group A Reader-card access, and directs manuscript consultation to the Weston Library.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Special collections mediated copying.
evidence.locator: How to order images, cost, turnaround, restrictions, and contact
evidence.summary: The service offers reference-quality PDF scans to all users, lists £1 per scan for non-Oxford users and an average 10-working-day turnaround, gives mediated.copying@bodleian.ox.ac.uk, and subjects images to Imaging Services Terms of Use and item-level exceptions.

evidence.citation: Folger Shakespeare Library, Facsimile of MS. Bodley 34, 1960.
evidence.summary: The catalog identifies the 1960 EETS OS 247 printed facsimile, N. R. Ker's introduction, its contents including Hali Meiðhad, pagination, and an open-stacks Folger copy.

evidence.citation: U.S. Copyright Office, Circular 15A, Duration of Copyright, revised April 2026.
evidence.locator: Works already under statutory protection before 1978
evidence.summary: The Copyright Office states that all works published in the United States before 1931 are in the public domain and explains renewal/term rules for later pre-1978 works.

open_questions.question: How should HMaid.xml's historical copying/redistribution notice be reconciled with U-M's current No Copyright–United States statement?
next_steps: Ask U-M whether the current item-level rights statement supersedes the embedded 1997 availability notice for redistribution of the digital transcription.
Preserve both dated statements in provenance rather than silently deleting the older notice.

open_questions.question: Has Bodley 34 been fully digitized for public access, apart from the 1960 printed facsimile and modern edited text?
next_steps: Ask Bodleian Special Collections whether an unindexed or staff-only digital surrogate exists.
Request reference scans of fols. 52v–71v and written reuse terms if a public object is unavailable.
Consult the 1960 printed facsimile for layout comparison.

open_questions.question: When will the British Library's digital images for Cotton Titus D XVIII become available again, and under what reuse terms?
next_steps: Recheck the interim Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue and legacy Digitised Manuscripts link periodically.
Ask British Library Reference Services whether images of fols. 112v–127r can currently be supplied and request written reuse terms.

open_questions.question: Which physical or microfilm copy of Furnivall's 1922 volume did U-M scan, and were any corrections made against another copy?
next_steps: Ask U-M whether scanning logs, source-copy identifiers, OCR correction files, or QA documentation survive.
Compare HMaid.xml against the HathiTrust page images, treating witness text, modernization, and notes separately.
